coredata.dc:description: Ions of mercury, one of the most hazardous heavy metals in nature, pose serious risks to the environment and human health. Blue sulfur-doped carbon dots (SCDs) from corn stalks were utilized as material. The SCDs were incorporated into a carboxylated hydrogel modified with sulfur, and a compound gel (SCDs–KTOCS gel) was successfully fabricated for simultaneous fluorescence detection and Hg(II) adsorption. This enabled the effective identification and removal of Hg(II) from contaminated water. The chemical content, fluorescence properties, and adsorption behaviors of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels were analyzed. The results demonstrate that the SCDs–KTOCS–gels exhibited effective Hg(II) adsorption (193 mg/g) and an extensive linear spectrum for Hg(II) fluorescence emission (150–500 mg/L; detection limit = 1.5668 mg/L). The adsorption values fit well with the Temkin models and pseudo-second-order kinetics. Additionally, Hg(II) detection and adsorption in the SCDs–KTOCS–gels were examined. By exchanging the existing probe for a suitable one that fits various relevant applications, this study suggests an environmentally friendly and sustainable method of producing materials for removing and detecting Hg(II) and constructing a fluorescence hydrogel for the detection and adsorption of different metals.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent cellulose hydrogels based on corn stalk of double sulfhydryl functional group modification for Hg(II) removal and detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ions of mercury, one of the most hazardous heavy metals in nature, pose serious risks to the environment and human health. Blue sulfur-doped carbon dots (SCDs) from corn stalks were utilized as material. The SCDs were incorporated into a carboxylated hydrogel modified with sulfur, and a compound gel (SCDs–KTOCS gel) was successfully fabricated for simultaneous fluorescence detection and Hg(II) adsorption. This enabled the effective identification and removal of Hg(II) from contaminated water. The chemical content, fluorescence properties, and adsorption behaviors of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels were analyzed. The results demonstrate that the SCDs–KTOCS–gels exhibited effective Hg(II) adsorption (193 mg/g) and an extensive linear spectrum for Hg(II) fluorescence emission (150–500 mg/L; detection limit = 1.5668 mg/L). The adsorption values fit well with the Temkin models and pseudo-second-order kinetics. Additionally, Hg(II) detection and adsorption in the SCDs–KTOCS–gels were examined. By exchanging the existing probe for a suitable one that fits various relevant applications, this study suggests an environmentally friendly and sustainable method of producing materials for removing and detecting Hg(II) and constructing a fluorescence hydrogel for the detection and adsorption of different metals.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The natural environment has been considerably impacted by effluent from various industries, which include electrolysis, pesticides, pharmaceuticals, paper manufacturing, electroplating, printing and dyeing, and textiles. This is because organic pollutants and heavy metal ions in industrial effluents are persistent and do not break down naturally [1]. The World Health Organization has designated mercury ions a priority-regulated pollutant because Hg-containing substances can be extremely harmful even at considerably low concentrations [2]. Bioaccumulation of Hg in animals and the amount of Hg in the diet are important [3]. If Hg levels exceed safe limits, health risks, including renal, immune system, nervous system, and cardiovascular damage [4,5] may arise. The adsorption of metal ions or organic pollutants is impacted by several conventional techniques, such as the Fenton response method [6,7], biological treatments [8,9], membrane technologies [10], extraction [11], electrical dialysis [12], and materials, such as porous organic polymers [13], cyclic olefin polymers [14], and covalent organic frameworks [15]. Nevertheless, polymer gels are typically effective and adaptable soft substances with considerable potential in various applications, which include drug delivery and engineering [16–20], drug delivery matrices [21], cell-modulating agents [22], and heavy metal adsorption [23–25]. The network configuration of hydrogels offers a three-dimensional (3D) space with excellent water retention properties, adjustable mechanical properties, and the ability to incorporate nutrients [26], making them suitable for various environmental applications.

Cellulose is a widely distributed and abundant polysaccharide found in nature, often derived from biomass waste [27,28]. It comprises several β-glucose molecules, and each glucose molecule is bound to each other via β-1,4 glycosidic bonds to form a long chain of macromolecular substances. In cellulose molecules, the chemical bonds connecting the glucose molecules are in different directions, forming a chain without branches or curling. Therefore, using cellulose as a raw material for modifying specific functional groups can ensure its stable structural properties, which provide a stable cellulose backbone structure for the subsequent preparation of hydrogels [29]. Additionally, cellulose is a helpful material in water treatment for eliminating metals owing to its biodegradability, nontoxicity, renewability, and numerous hydrogel groups that interact electrostatically with heavy metals. To adsorb Hg(II) ions [30,31], created a unique chemically modified cellulose (Gu–MC) material with absorption capacity of 48 mg/g. Using a straightforward chemical process, cellulose fluorescence probes with BODIPY were created, enabling the option identification and elimination of Hg2+/Hg2 2+. The adsorption capacity were 164.2 and 159.4 mg/g [32]. Owing to its low flexibility, cellulose is rarely employed alone in water treatment applications. Instead, various techniques have been explored to increase its performance [33,34].

Numerous nanomaterials can be integrated into the cellulose-based hydrogel matrix using the aforementioned methods [35,36]. It is anticipated that certain nanomaterials, including carbon dots (CDs) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO)–oxidized cellulose nanofibers (TOCN), will be utilized to optimize the performance of cellulose-based hydrogels. The many carboxyl groups found in TOCN are typically formed by oxidizing primary hydroxyl groups [37]. Furthermore, TOCN contributes to enhancing adsorption effectiveness owing to its intrinsic qualities, such as biodegradability, strong dispersion stability, high structural rigidity, ease of network structure construction, and an ample heavy metal binding site [38]. Furthermore, the strong fluorescence, biocompatibility, and lack of toxicity of CDs have made them a popular choice for chemical and biological sensors [39,40]. The strong fluorescence characteristic indicates considerable promise for novel uses in analytical chemistry, particularly in environmental sensing applications, such as heavy metal detection. Therefore, adding CDs can help cellulose-based hydrogels develop novel functions [41]. This research mainly aims to create an innovative cellulose-based gel with enhanced adsorption capacity and additional features by leveraging the beneficial properties of TOCN and CDs.

This study developed and investigated an innovative fluorescent cellulose-based hydrogel containing sulfur-doped CDs (SCDs) and KTOCS. Blue-emitting carbon quantum dots (SCDs) prepared hydrothermally from carboxylated straw cellulose using 1,3-propanedithiol as a raw material for the first timeThe adsorption characteristics of Hg(II) onto this hydrogel were examined. The synthesis of the SCDs–KTOCS gels for Hg(II) removal and detection is shown in Scheme 1 .which not only exhibits efficient adsorption andremoval of mercury ions but also demonstrates a highly selective fluorescence response to mercury in aqueous solutions. The models of balance isotherms and adsorption kinetics were assessed. Moreover, the sensitivity and selectivity of the fluorescent detection method for Hg(II) were examined. Based on this, the mechanism for the highly effective Hg(II) adsorption and detection by these new cellulose-based hydrogels was identified. The dual- functional performance enhances the material's practical application by enabling rapid and sensitive detection of mercury contamination.

Cornstalk (CS) was procured from Dalat Banner, Ordos City, Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region, China, and used in this research. After crushing and drying, a 100–120 mesh powder was prepared. To crosslink the KTOCS and SCDs, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), urea, and epichlorohydrin were used. Sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), hydrochloric acid (HCl), acetic acid (HAC, 99.5 %), and NaOH were used in the bleaching of CS. To oxidize the hydroxyl position of the CS carboxyl group, sulfuric acid, TEMPO, and sodium bromide (NaBr) were used. Nitric acid (HNO3), sodium borohydride, sodium chloride (NaCl), and iron(III) chloride hexahydrate were acquired from Aladdin, China. Ethanol (C2H5OH), manganese chloride, lead nitrate, barium chloride, copper sulfate, chromium nitrate, magnesium sulfate, cadmium nitrate, zinc sulfate, calcium chloride, nickel acetate, 1,3-propanedithiol, mercury sulfate (HgSO4), ammonium iron(II) sulfate, and 3-mercaptopropyl trimethoxysilane (KH590) were acquired from Macklin, China.

First, 10,000 g of 200-mesh CS straw was weighed and placed in a three-necked flask containing a mixture of HNO3–C2H5OH in a 1:3 ratio (material ratio 1:43 g/L). The flask was placed in a 90 °C water bath for 3 h. After the reaction, the material was dried at 100 °C and cleaned with water and C2H5OH until neutral. The obtained samples were dried and treated with a 7.5 % sodium chlorite solution at a 1:20 mg/L material ratio. The pH was adjusted to 3–4 using HAC, and the mixture was incubated in a water bath at 75 °C for 3 h. Afterward, the material was washed with deionized (DI) water and C2H5OH to allow it to dry to a consistent weight. As the bleaching of cellulose using sodium chlorite is selective oxidation, it only oxidizes the potential aldehyde group at the end of the cellulose molecular chain without affecting the strength of the cellulose. The removal of lignin increases the porosity of cellulose, enhancing its adsorption and fluorescent probe sites. Following bleaching, the samples were immersed in a potassium hydroxide solution at a 1:20-g/mL ratio and stirred magnetically for 2 h at 75 °C, followed by drying at 60 °C. Then, 0.603 g of NaBr and 0.032 g of TEMPO were added to the samples agitation at 60 °C for 30 min. After adding 20 mL of NaClO, the pH of the mixture was adjusted to between 10.0 and 10.5 using a 0.5-M HAC solution before being neutralized with water and C2H5OH–water solution. The main purpose is to oxidize the hydroxyl group at position 6 of the cellulose to a carboxyl group, enabling subsequent sulfhydrylation modifications. This process results in TEMPO-oxidized cornstalk cellulose (TOCS).

Furthermore, 10,000 g of TOCS suspension with a concentration of 10 wt% was mixed in 99 % C2H5OH (100 mL) and agitated at 200 r/min for 3 min. The pH of the TOCS was adjusted to 4.0 using HCl. To create a modified TOCS suspension, 0.1 g of KH590 was added to the TOCS suspension, and 3 h later, it was ultrasonically diffused. After removing the solvent, the suspension was centrifuged for 10 min to produce pure KH590-modified TOCS (KTOCS). It was cleaned three times with DI water and dried in a vacuum oven.

Next, KTOCS, urea, NaOH, and DI water were mixed in a beaker based on a material ratio of 4:7:12:81 and placed in the refrigerator for 30 min at 20 °C to ensure full dissolution of cellulose. After the reaction, the mixture was removed and thawed at room temperature before adding 9 % ECH. The polymerization process was crosslinked to 55 °C for 2 h. After crosslinking, the resulting hydrogel was washed with DI water for 48 h to remove the unreacted chemicals. Impregnating the generated KTOCS gels in 0.5-g/L SCDs was the next step. The SCDs–KTOCS gels were produced by freeze-drying the fluorescent aerogel and flushing it three times with DI water after 48 h (Scheme 2 ).
Following the formation of a homogeneous solution by stirring a mixture of 2.0-g TOCS and 0.5-mL 1,3-propanedithiol into 60-mL DI water, the mixture was treated hydrothermally in an autoclave for.
16 h at 180 °C. After the reaction, the solution was filtered through a dialysis membrane for a full day. Finally, SCDs were obtained using a freezing and drying oven.
First, a four-way cuvette was filled with 3 mL of SCDs. It was then filled with Hg(II) solutions at several concentrations, and the fluorescence measurements were recorded.

The limit of detection (LOD) of the SCDs and SCDs–KTOCS-gels can be determined using the following formula:

where K is the slope of the standard curve and σ is the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity for the empty sample.

To summarize, 50 mL of Hg(II) and 0.05 g of SCDs–KTOCS–gels were placed in 100-mL conical flasks, and an adsorption experiment was conducted using a thermostatic shaker. After adsorption equilibration, the liquid obtained was filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane, and the filtrate was evaluated using an atomic fluorescence spectrometer. To ascertain the adsorption of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels, the effects of pH (1–6), contact length (0–120 min), initial Hg(II) concentration (50–500 mg/L), and temperatures (298, 303, 308, 313, 318, and 323 K) were evaluated. The Hg(II) adsorption capabilities (Q e) of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels were determined using the following formula:

Research on adsorption was conducted at 298, 303, 308, 313, 318, and 323 K to examine the temperature effects on the ability of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels to adsorb Hg(II). At room temperature, the regeneration performance of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels was investigated. A 50-mL solution of 400-mg/L Hg(II) was mixed with 50 mg of adsorbents and left for 2 h. The adsorbents were then submerged in 50 mL of 1-mol/L HCl solution and shaken at 298 K for 2 h to remove and regenerate the SCDs–KTOCS–gels Hg(II). Afterward, the adsorbents were washed with DI water. The SCDs–KTOCS–gels were reused for an additional adsorption cycle.
Detailed characterization methods are provided in the Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical properties of SCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showed that the SCDs were round and equally distributed (Fig. 1a). The SCDs exhibited a crystal structure with an average dimension of 35 nm and a lattice distance of 0.22 nm (Figs. 1b and c). This suggests that SCDs have satisfactory crystallography. Fig. 1d displays the Raman spectra of SCDs. The peak at 1360 cm−1 is known as the disordered band. The notable peak in the G band that developed at about 1589 cm−1 corresponds to the G band of Raman [45,46]. Regarding structure, the SCDs resembled graphite with an ID/IG ratio of 0.88, indicating an increased degree of graphitization [47]. The surface function observed on the SCDs was investigated using Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The OH stretching vibrations can be linked to the prominent peaks in the FTIR spectrum of the SCDs (Fig. 1e) at 3300–3500 cm−1.

The peaks at 2925 cm−1 were created by CH stretching vibrations. The presence of SH in SCDs was indicated by the peak detected at 2528 cm−1. The C–O–C bonds were confirmed by a peak around 1040 cm−1 [48]. The peaks at 668 cm−1 were created by the CS stretching vibrations. In Fig. 1f, for the SCDs 13C NMR, the peaks at 20–40 ppm are related to CS bonds, whereas the signals at 60–100 ppm are characteristic peaks for cellulose [49].

To determine the chemical composition of the SCDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrogram analysis was used. Differential peaks at 163, 285, and 532 eV were identified and assigned to sulfur 2p (S 2p), carbon 1 s (C 1s), and oxygen 1 s (O 1s), respectively (Fig. 2a). The spectra of SCDs for the C, O, and S atom bond configuration analysis are displayed in Fig. 2b. The peaks observed at 284.8, 286.3, and 288.1 eV correspond to the CC, C–O/C–S, and O–C=O bonds, respectively, indicating that 1,3-propanedithiol was successfully introduced into SCDs during their preparation. The O 1s spectra of the SCDs are displayed in Fig. 2c. Peaks at 531.5 and 532 eV correspond to CO and CO bonds, respectively. The high-resolution S 2p spectrum of the SCDs is displayed in Fig. 2d. Peaks at 162.3 and 163.4 eV correspond to SH and CS bonds, respectively. The successful preparation of the SCDs was confirmed via XPS spectroscopy.

Peaks at 230 and 270 nm were visible in the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum of the SCD solutions (Fig. 3a). These absorbent peaks were induced by n–π* and CC π–π* transitions involving oxygen- or nitrogen-containing groups as well as sp2 aromatic domains [50,51]. In response to stimulation at 359 nm, a powerful peak emerged at a maximum emission of 436 nm. The 200-mg/L SCD solution changed from colorless to brilliant blue on exposure to 365-nm UV light, as seen in Fig. 3b. Fig. 3c shows the emission spectrum of SCDs at 310–400 nm excitation. At 400 nm, the highest degree of fluorescence was seen. The figure shows that with increasing excitation wavelength, the emission wavelength undergoes a redshift. The fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength of carbon-based fluorescent materials typically depend on excitation [52–54]. This is primarily attributed to the emission wavelength produced by the dispersion of particle sizes [55]. As shown in Fig. 3d, the relationship between the excitation and emission wavelengths of SCDs was readily apparent by the 3D fluorescent spectrogram. The surface imperfections of SCDs show a substantial link with photoluminescence (PL), which may circumvent the size depending on interband conversions [56]. Applications based on fluorescence are favorable for PL stability. Therefore, tests were carried out on the fluorescent stability of the SCD solution at different NaCl concentrations, temperatures, times, and pH levels. Fig. S1 shows the effect of ambient temperature on the stability of SCDs, and it can be concluded that SCDs still exhibit satisfactory fluorescence stability when the temperature reaches 85 °C. Fig. S2 demonstrates the effect of SCD storage time on the fluorescence intensity, and the fluorescence intensity of SCD solution will gradually decrease with increasing storage time, mainly attributed to the oxidation of –SH in SCDs exposed to air. At increased NaCl concentrations of up to 1 mol/L, the SCD solution demonstrated remarkable PL stability (Fig. 3e). Next, we tested the fluorescence stability properties of SCDs at pH levels ranging from 1 to 11. The fluorescence intensity showed no noticeable changes between pH 5 and 9 (Fig. 3f). The substantial suppression of fluorescence observed in the presence of strong bases (pH = 11) and strong acids (pH = 1, 3) could be attributed to SCD protonation in an acidic environment and their surface reaction with –SH in an alkaline environment [57]. To assess their selectivity, the PL spectroscopy of the SCDs produced under the influence of various metal ions was analyzed. A total of 100 μg of SCDs was reacted with various metal cations, such as Ba(II), Hg(II), Ni(II), Ca(II), Cd(II), Zn(II), Mn(II), Cu(II), Fe(II), Mg(II), Cr(III), and Pb(II) (Fig. 3g). Under UV illumination at 359 nm, the PL spectra of the SCDs containing different Hg(II) ions are shown in Fig. 3h. As evident from the figure, the fluorescence intensity of SCDs decreases with increasing Hg(II) concentration. The slope of the curve representing the linear relationship between the peak intensity of PL ((F 0
F)/F 0) and Hg(II) yielded a correlation value of 0.9938, indicating excellent linearity (Fig. 3i).

Fig. 4a shows the preparation process of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels. Fig. 4b and c present scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the freeze-dried SCDs–KTOCS–gels, showing a 3D network structure that illustrates the interconnected framework of the SCDs with attached KTOCS gels. Subsequent magnification showed that connected, wall-shaped KTOCS gels comprised most of the skeleton of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels. The FTIR spectra of KTOCS and TOCS are displayed in Fig. S3. The –OH stretching vibration was visible in the FTIR spectra of both TOCS and KTOCS. The peaks at 2927 and 2898 cm−1 corresponded to symmetric and asymmetric CH stretching vibrations, which are characteristic of cellulose [58]. Following the fiber modification, the absorption peak at 1040 cm−1 increased, possibly attributed to the antisymmetric stretching vibrations of the newly formed Si–O–Si bonds overlapping. Additional peaks in the KTOCS spectrum at 1240 and 668 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of the SiC and CS bonds, respectively [59]. These demonstrate that the KTOCS was created from KH590 and effectively grafted onto the TOCS surface. The FTIR spectra of the SCDs, KTOCS, and SCDs–KTOCS–gels are displayed in Fig. 4d. The strong CO absorption band, which shifts from 1700 cm−1 in the SCDs to 1730 cm−1 in the SCDs–KTOCS–gels, is observed in the spectra again, suggesting the existence of an ester group [60]. This result implies that the sulfhydryl-rich hydrothermally generated SCDs condensed the carboxyl groups of KTOCS to make sulfhydryl. These SCDs were then embedded to function for the SCDs–KTOCS–gels [61] [62]. In addition, the SCDs–KTOCS–gels contain the 668 cm−1C–S bond from the SCDs and the 1240 cm−1 SiC bond from the KTOCS. Every spectrum displayed –OH stretching vibrations between 3000 and 3500 cm−1, with the SCDs–KTOCS–gels showing a stronger band than the KTOCS. This variation in intensity is attributed to the presence of sulfhydryl groups on the surface of SCDs. The –SH groups on the surface of SCDs can form covalent bonds with the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels via a nucleophilic reaction [63]. Fig. S4 shows the Raman spectra of the SCDs, KTOCS gels, and SCDs–KTOCS–gels. From the figure, it is evident that the Raman intensity of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels is larger than that of the KTOCS gels, indicating the successful introduction of SCDs into the SCDs–KTOCS–gels. Fig. S5 displays the 13C NMR spectra of the KTOCS gels. The samples show the typical characteristic cellulose peaks: C1 (105.8 ppm), C4 (83.5 ppm), and C2, C3, and C5 (75.7 ppm). The carboxyl group from cellulose is for the peaks at C6 (179.5 ppm). The signals at 50.5, 27.9, and 12.8 ppm in the KTOCS spectra are obtained from KH590's C7, C8, and C9, confirming that KH590 was successfully grafted onto the KTOCS surface.

Tables S1 and S2 present the specific surface area and elemental analyses of SCDs–KTOCS–gels, respectively, detailing the specific surface area, pore size, radius, and elemental content. According to Table S1, the SCDs–KTOCS–gels have a high specific surface area, pore volume, and pore radius. As shown in Table S2, the SCDs–KTOCS–gels contain 1.95 % elemental S, confirming the successful preparation of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels.

Notably, XPS was used to investigate the SCDs–KTOCS–gels to determine their chemical composition. Different peaks at 284.8, 163, and 532 eV corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, and S 2p, respectively, are shown in Fig. 4e. Fig. 4f and g display the high-resolution C 1s and O 1s spectra used to examine the bond configurations of the C and O atoms in the SCDs–KTOCS–gels. The CC, C–O/C-S, and CO peaks were present at 284.8, 286.4, and 288.1 eV, respectively. When comparing the spectra of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels to those in Fig. S2, the CO peak of KTOCS shifted from 289 to 288 eV. This occurred because the ester groups in the SCDs–KTOCS–gels caused a shift in peak position values. As Fig. 2d illustrates, the fine spectrum of the S peak of SCDs comprises two peaks of CS (162.3 eV) and SH (163.4 eV). This showed the creation of ester groups through dehydration condensation between the sulfhydryl groups on the surface of the SCDs and the carboxyl groups on the KTOCS surface. It also revealed that SCDs were made via a hydrothermal reaction, resulting in S-doped carbocation cores and abundant sulfhydryl and carboxyl groups on the surface. A couple of peaks at 530.5 and 532.1 eV were found and correlated with the CO and CO bonds. These peaks may be used to fit the O 1s spectrum of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels. The successful preparation of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels was further confirmed by XPS spectroscopy.

Fig. 5 displays the 13C NMR spectra of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels. The samples show the typical characteristic cellulose peaks: C1 (104.8 ppm), C4 (84.3 ppm), and C2, C3, and C5 (74.8 ppm). The ester group in cellulose corresponds to the peak at C6 (179.3 ppm). The signals at 36.8, 14.9, and 14.4 ppm in the KTOCS spectra correspond to KH590's C10, C11, and C12[50]. The signals at 32.9, 23.4, and 32.5 ppm in the SCD–KTOCS gel spectra are associated with the introduction of C7, C8, and C9, respectively, in SCDs, verifying that KH590 and SCDs were successfully grafted onto the KTOCS surface.

A schematic of the quenching of SCDs–KTOCS–gels submerged in 400-mg/L Hg (II) solution is shown in Fig. 6a. The graph shows that after immersion in Hg(II), fluorescence quenching occurred in the SCDs–KTOCS–gels, suggesting that Hg(II) exhibited a favorable quenching effect. The SEM images of Hg(II) adsorption by the SCDs–KTOCS–gels are displayed in Fig. 6b and c. The Hg (II) appeared to be evenly distributed within the SCDs–KTOCS–gels. Fig. 6d displays the FTIR spectra of the SCD–KTOCS gel–Hg(II) and the SCDs–KTOCS–gels. Following Hg(II) adsorption, the OH bands of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels decreased. This result showed that the hydrogel groups acted as adsorption sites for the Hg(II) ions. The stretching vibration of the HgS bonds is the additional peak at 1375 cm−1 in the SCDs–KTOCS–gels spectra following Hg(II) addition. Meanwhile, the CO stretching vibrational peak at 1600 cm−1 disappears. This outcome demonstrated the reaction between the sulfhydryl group and Hg(II) (Fig. 6e–h). The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) spectra of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels before and after Hg(II) adsorption are displayed in Fig. 6i–m. From the figure, the three elements of C, O, and S are uniformly distributed on the surface of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels. The effective adsorption of Hg(II) by the SCDs–KTOCS–gels is further supported by the change in atomic percentage observed in the SCDs–KTOCS–gels before and after adsorption, as shown in Table S3.

The fluorescence responses of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels to Hg(II) are shown in Fig. S7. The figure demonstrates a broad concentration limit of 150–500 mg/L, indicating a strong linear relation (R 2 = 0.9848) between the concentration of Hg(II) and the relative PL peak intensity (F 0
F)/F 0 of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels. For the SCDs–KTOCS–gels, the detection limit for Hg(II) was 1.5668 mg/L. The fluorescence intensity gradient of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels is shown in Fig. S7 on the right side, indicating the progressive fluorescence quenching of the gels under various Hg(II) concentrations. Based on the fluorescence intensity shown in Fig. S8, it can be concluded that the detection of Hg(II) is unaffected by other metal ions. Thus, the SCDs–KTOCS–gels can be applied to selectively and sensitively recognize Hg(II) from fluorescence without any interference.

To determine the optimal Hg(II) adsorption performance of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels, four factors were examined: initial concentration, pH, temperature, and adsorption period. From Fig. 7a, it is evident that the optimal initial concentration of adsorbed Hg(II) was 400 mg/L. This is likely because as the concentration of Hg(II) ions increased, the number of Hg(II) ions in the solution rose, thus increasing the mutual collisions with the adsorption sites in the SCDs–KTOCS–gels and, consequently, an increase in adsorption. However, if the concentration of Hg(II) ions in the initial solution was further increased, the effective active adsorption sites of the SCD–KTOCS gel adsorption materials became saturated with Hg(II) ions, and the amount of adsorption stabilized. The optimum adsorption temperature of 40 °C is indicated in Fig. 7b, and it is evident that the adsorption of Hg(II) ions by the SCDs–KTOCS–gels shows a tendency to increase and decrease with increasing temperature of the system solution. This is because as the adsorption temperature of the system increases, the hydrogen bonding between the long polymer chains in the structure of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels partially weakens, thus enhancing the number of activated adsorption sites and adsorption capacity accordingly. This facilitates the adsorption of the Hg(II) ions onto the SCDs–KTOCS–gels. However, if the temperature continues to increase, the adsorption process, which is exothermic, will eventually weaken, resulting in a decrease in the adsorption amount. As shown in Fig. 7c, the optimum adsorption pH is 4. This is because when the solution pH value is low, the concentration of H+ ions in the solution is high. When Hg(II) ions and the active adsorption sites in the SCDs–KTOCS–gels come into contact, the H+ ions also compete for these sites, which somewhat inhibits the adsorption of Hg(II) ions. When the pH value is 4, the H+ concentration decreases, and the adsorption capacity of the active adsorption sites of the SCD–KTOCS gel adsorbent material for Hg(II) ions reaches the maximum value. If the solution pH value is higher than 4, Hg(II) ions are prone to phenomena such as complexation and precipitation with alkaline pH modifiers, leading to a decrease in adsorption capacity. Therefore, the optimum pH value of the initial Hg(II) solution was 4. The optimum adsorption time of 90 min is shown in Fig. 7d. This figure shows that the adsorption of Hg(II) ions by the SCDs–KTOCS–gels initially increases rapidly and stabilizes with prolonged adsorption time. This occurs because Hg(II) ions first diffuse from the system solution to the surface of the adsorbent, then into its internal pores, and finally bind to the active adsorption sites in the structure of the adsorbent to reach equilibrium. As adsorption time increases, the active adsorption sites of the adsorbent SCDs–KTOCS–gels gradually reach saturation, and the adsorption amount stabilizes.

As shown in Fig. S9, the zeta potential analysis also reveals a maximum negative value when pH is 4 and Hg(II) is positively charged. Since mercury ions are positively charged and SCDs-KTOCS-gels show maximum negative values at pH 4. This characterization indirectly confirms that the maximum adsorption pH of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels is 4. Furthermore, the maximum adsorption was 193 mg/g. Table 1 compares the Hg(II) adsorption capabilities of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels with those of other biomass-based adsorbents reported in previous studies. Based on the findings, the SCDs–KTOCS–gels have a high adsorption capacity, suggesting their effectiveness as adsorbents for Hg(II).

A comprehensive analysis of the system was performed using a kinetic study. The SCDs–KTOCS–gels reached equilibrium in about 90 min, as illustrated in Fig. 7e. A coefficient of a nonlinear relationship (R 2 = 0.9901) was obtained as the rate data were modeled based on pseudo-second-order, as shown in Fig. 7e and S10–11. Adsorption modeling aligned closely with the pseudo-secondary model. However, the pseudo-first-order model showed a poor fit [71,72]. Table S4 displays a list of the precise fitting parameters. Consequently, the pseudo-second-order model is followed by the adsorption process. The fitting findings demonstrated that boundary layer resistance was not the rate-limiting step [73]. However, the adsorption amount in the pseudo-first-order models is close to the measured value, indicating that the material relies on the chemisorption of monomolecular layers and the synergistic physical adsorption of Hg(II) [74]. Additionally, during Hg(II) capture, chemical adsorption was common [75]. To clarify how the adsorbents and adsorbate interacted, an isotherm model was then employed. The adsorption capacity rose with the concentration and eventually reached saturation. The results fitted to the Langmuir, Dubinin–Radushkevich, Temkin, and Freundlich models are shown in Figs. 7f and S10–14, and Table S5 contains a list of the precise fitting parameters. As shown in Table S3, the R 2 value of the Temkin model is higher than that of the other three isothermal adsorption models. The fitting results agree with the Temkin isothermal adsorption model, indicating that the Temkin model effectively controls the adsorption behavior of a wide range of reactive substances. To perform the adsorption/desorption cycle studies, the SCDs–KTOCS–gels were regenerated by treatment with 1-mol/L HCl solution for 2 h. The effect of cyclic adsorption of Hg(II) by SCD–KTOCS gel materials is shown in Fig. S8. It can be concluded that the SCDs–KTOCS–gels can be reused four times under optimal adsorption conditions, with the adsorption amount each time exceeding 110 mg/g. However, there is a marked decrease compared to the original adsorption amount, primarily owing to the strong stability of the HgS bond, which is not easy to dissolve by HCI, whereas only the HgO bond reacts with HCl. Based on the above experimental findings, it can be demonstrated that the adsorption process conforms to the Temkin isothermal adsorption model of multinomial adsorption.

To evaluate the adsorption potential of SCDs–KTOCS–gels, the impact of temperature on their stability was investigated. Table 2 and Fig. S16 show the temperature-dependent increase in adsorption capacity from 293 to 323 K. The adsorption process was understood by establishing thermodynamic parameters. The positive ΔH 0 value indicates that Hg(II) removal is endothermic and that entropy dominates Hg(II) adsorption [76]. Every value of ΔG 0 was negative. The negative ΔG 0 values increased with rising temperature, suggesting that adsorption was enhanced at high temperatures [77]. Therefore, the porous structure and various functional groups most likely aid in eliminating Hg(II) during adsorption.

The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 8a) revealed that each surface of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels had peaks for many chemical components following adsorption. Fig. 8b–d display the high-resolution spectra of Hg 4f, C 1s, and S 2p before and after the SCDs–KTOCS–gels adsorption. The SCDs–KTOCS gels–Hg(II) surface had two valence states of Hg: Hg(0) and Hg(II) [78]. Three Hg 4f peaks at 100.1, 100.6, and 104.4 eV may be obtained by curve fitting the raw data. We hypothesize that the peaks at 100.1 and 100.6 eV correspond to black HgO and red HgS, respectively [79–81]. This notion was further corroborated by the C 1s and S 2p spectra displayed in Fig. 8c and d. The CO signal in the C 1s spectra at 287.5 eV was noticeably reduced in the Hg(II) adsorbed by the SCDs–KTOCS–gels. Concurrently, the presence of HgS caused the chemical shift of CO to migrate from 285.5 to 288.4 eV. In the S 2p spectra, a novel peak owing to HgS was observed in the Hg(II) adsorbed by the SCDs–KTOCS–gels at 161.5 eV. The typical peak of the SO species at approximately 168–169 eV indicates that HgSO4 has been adsorbed onto the surface of the material.

These findings suggest that the 3D fluorescence SCDs–KTOCS–gels provided in Scheme 3 have an adsorption and sensing mechanism. The CO stretching vibration peak at 1600 cm−1 of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels before and after adsorption disappeared in FTIR spectroscopy. This indicates that the sulfhydryl group reacts with Hg(II). The Hg(II) ions specifically bond with the thiol group when the Hg(II) ion solution enters the SCD–KTOCS gel material. This is because they both follow the lowest energy principle [82], where the electrons are arranged in the outermost energy level of the atom, and Hg(II), with an outermost electron arrangement of 6s0, contains a vacant orbital. However, the S atom in the CS bond contains a lone pair of electrons. Consequently, the lone pair on CS combines with the empty orbital on Hg(II), producing compounds of coordinated Hg(II) and SCDs–KTOCS–gels [83]. The mixture causes the fluorescence of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels to be quenched. The soft and hard acid-base hypotheses proposed by Pearson [84] and Wu [85] also explain the bonding mechanism: CS is a soft base that may readily form a bond with a soft acid, and Hg(II) is a soft acid.

Additionally, the porous 3D structure of the hydrogel facilitates the passage of Hg(II) ions from the exterior to the interior by offering Hg(II) ion transport channels. Thus, the interior areas of the hydrogel absorb Hg(II) ions, which are then attached to the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of the hydrogel. Consequently, the remarkable adsorption capability of Hg(II) is demonstrated by the 3D porous fluorescent hydrogel. Adding Hg(II) ions causes the SCDs to adsorb the ions because of their specific interaction with the sulfhydryl groups, leading to rapid fluorescence of the ions. Consequently, the SCDs–KTOCS–gels exhibited sensitive fluorescence in response to Hg(II) [86].

We prepared a fluorescent hydrogel composite of mercapto-modified CS cellulose for detection and adsorption of Hg(II) in water bodies. Blue carbon quantum dots were generated from CS as raw materials and doped into the inner KTOCS gels, and a mixed hydrogel with combined detection and adsorption capabilities was successfully fabricated. The synthesis, form, chemical makeup, and optical properties of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels were investigated. The gel network showed a crosslinked 3D structure in which ester bonds were used to crosslink KTOCS gels and SCDs. When exposed to UV light, the SCDs–KTOCS–gels produced stable blue fluorescence. A thorough investigation was carried out into the fluorescence response and Hg(II) adsorption capability of SCDs–KTOCS–gels. The SCDs–KTOCS–gels exhibited an extensive variety of linear fluorescence responses for Hg(II) (0–500 mg/L) because of the nonradiative energy transition between SCDs and Hg(II). The S and O groups in the hydrogel interacted with Hg(II) chemically and physically, resulting in a high Hg(II) adsorption capacity of 193 mg/g for the SCDs–KTOCS–gels. These results collectively demonstrate the considerable potential of the SCDs–KTOCS–gels for the adsorption and monitoring of heavy metal ions.

Boyun Wang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Wanqi Zhang: Project administration, Investigation. Yuan Guo: Data curation. Ximing Wang: Writing – original draft, Resources. Xiaotao Zhang: Writing – review & editing.
